facts.text: Kelly Slater holds the record for the most WSL World Championship titles with 11, spanning from 1992 to 2011.
The Banzai Pipeline on Oahu's North Shore is considered the most famous and dangerous wave in the world, breaking over a shallow reef.
Surfing was added to the Olympic program for the first time at the Tokyo 2020 Games, held at Tsurigasaki Beach.
Ancient Hawaiians called surfing 'he'e nalu,' which translates to 'wave sliding,' and it was deeply embedded in their culture and spirituality.
A shortboard is typically between 5'6" and 6'4" in length and is designed for high-performance surfing with sharp turns and aerial maneuvers.
Nazaré, Portugal, is home to the largest waves ever surfed, thanks to an underwater canyon that amplifies Atlantic swells.
Duke Kahanamoku, known as the father of modern surfing, introduced the sport to the world during the early 20th century while competing as an Olympic swimmer.
Waves are created by wind blowing across the surface of the ocean; the stronger the wind and the longer the fetch, the bigger the waves.
The Beach Boys' 1963 hit 'Surfin' U.S.A.' helped popularize surf culture across mainland America and beyond.
Bodyboarding was popularized by Tom Morey, who invented the Boogie Board in 1971 using polyethylene foam and a newspaper.
Mick Fanning famously fought off a great white shark during the 2015 J-Bay Open final in South Africa, escaping unharmed.
The WSL Championship Tour consists of approximately 10-12 events held at iconic surf breaks around the world each year.
Laird Hamilton is widely credited with pioneering tow-in surfing in the 1990s, using jet skis to access waves too large to paddle into.
A wetsuit works by trapping a thin layer of water between the suit and the skin, which the body heats up for insulation.
Jeffrey's Bay (J-Bay) in South Africa is renowned for its long, fast, perfectly shaped right-hand point break.
The first known depiction of surfing was found in cave paintings in Chan Chan, Peru, dating back over 2,000 years.
Stephanie Gilmore has won eight WSL Women's World Championship titles, making her one of the most decorated female surfers ever.
Reef breaks occur when ocean swells hit a coral reef or rocky bottom, creating hollow, powerful waves like Pipeline and Teahupo'o.
The Endless Summer (1966), directed by Bruce Brown, is considered the most influential surf film ever made, following two surfers on a global wave-hunting trip.
Channel Islands Surfboards, founded by Al Merrick in 1969, has been the board brand of choice for many world champions including Kelly Slater.
Rip currents are narrow channels of fast-moving water that flow away from shore and are responsible for the majority of surf-related rescues.
Gabriel Medina became the first Brazilian to win the WSL World Championship in 2014, sparking a Brazilian storm in professional surfing.
A longboard is typically 8 to 12 feet in length and offers a more classic, smooth style of surfing with nose riding and cross-stepping.
Teahupo'o in Tahiti produces one of the heaviest waves on the planet, breaking in extremely shallow water over a sharp coral reef.
Surf wax is applied to the deck of a surfboard to provide traction; different formulas are designed for cold, cool, warm, and tropical water temperatures.
Bethany Hamilton lost her left arm in a shark attack at age 13 in 2003 and returned to competitive surfing just one month later.
The Eddie Aikau Big Wave Invitational at Waimea Bay only runs when waves reach at least 20 feet on the face, making it one of the rarest contests in surfing.
Point breaks form when swells wrap around a headland or point of land, creating long, peeling waves ideal for surfing.
Jack O'Neill founded O'Neill Wetsuits in 1952 in San Francisco, helping make cold-water surfing possible and comfortable.
John John Florence won back-to-back WSL World Championship titles in 2016 and 2017, cementing his legacy as one of the best surfers of his generation.
The term 'hang ten' refers to a longboard maneuver where the surfer places all ten toes over the nose of the board.
Beach breaks occur when waves break over a sandy bottom, producing varying and shifting wave shapes depending on sandbars.
Dick Dale, known as the King of Surf Guitar, pioneered the surf rock genre with his 1962 hit 'Misirlou.'
In competitive surfing, rides are scored on a scale of 0 to 10 based on commitment, difficulty, speed, power, and flow.
Jaws (Pe'ahi) on Maui's North Shore is one of the most famous big wave spots in the world, with faces reaching up to 60 feet or more.
The fish surfboard design, characterized by its wide, flat shape and swallowtail, was originally developed by Steve Lis in the early 1970s.
Surfing's roots trace back over 3,000 years to Polynesian cultures, where riding waves was a central part of community life.
Mavericks, located near Half Moon Bay, California, is a legendary big wave break that remained a secret until the early 1990s.
Italo Ferreira of Brazil won the first-ever Olympic gold medal in surfing at the Tokyo 2020 Games.
A gun surfboard is a narrow, pointed board ranging from 7 to 12 feet, specifically designed for riding large, powerful waves.
The annual Pipe Masters at Pipeline on Oahu is considered the Super Bowl of surfing and the final stop of the WSL Championship Tour.
Uluwatu in Bali, Indonesia, is famous for its fast, hollow left-hand reef break and is a bucket-list destination for surfers worldwide.
Andy Irons won three consecutive WSL World Championship titles from 2002 to 2004 and was known for his fierce rivalry with Kelly Slater.
Hydrofoil surfboards use a wing-like foil beneath the board to lift the rider above the water, allowing surfing on very small waves.
The Rip Curl Pro at Bells Beach, Australia, is one of the longest-running professional surf contests, first held in 1962.
Swell is generated by storms far out at sea and can travel thousands of miles across the ocean before reaching the coastline as surfable waves.
Carissa Moore won four WSL Women's World Championship titles and the first Olympic gold medal in women's surfing at Tokyo 2020.
Trestles in San Clemente, California, is a world-class wave located in San Onofre State Beach and a regular WSL Championship Tour stop.
The movie Point Break (1991) featuring Keanu Reeves and Patrick Swayze brought surfing and its counterculture to mainstream cinema audiences.
Sebastian Zietz, Adriano de Souza, and Joel Parkinson are all surfers who have won WSL World Championship titles in highly competitive seasons.
Surfboard fins provide stability and control; the thruster (three-fin) setup, invented by Simon Anderson in 1981, became the standard for performance surfing.
Mike Stewart is considered the greatest bodyboarder of all time, having won nine IBA World Championship titles.
Huntington Beach, California, is known as 'Surf City USA' and hosts the annual US Open of Surfing each summer.
The leash, also called a leg rope, was invented in the 1970s and dramatically changed surfing by preventing boards from washing to shore after wipeouts.
Firewire Surfboards pioneered the use of sustainable materials like paulownia wood and bio-resins in modern surfboard construction.
Cloudbreak in Fiji is a powerful left-hand reef break accessible only by boat, producing some of the most spectacular waves in the South Pacific.
The concept of wave period refers to the time between successive wave crests; longer periods generally mean more powerful, organized waves.
Blue Crush (2002) was a surfing film that popularized women's surfing and was partially inspired by the real-life experiences of female surfers in Hawaii.
Tom Curren, a three-time world champion from Santa Barbara, California, is considered one of the most stylish surfers in history.
A beach break's sandbar formations can shift with storms and tides, meaning the quality of waves at beach breaks can change dramatically over time.
In 2011, Garrett McNamara surfed a wave at Nazaré, Portugal, estimated at 78 feet, setting a Guinness World Record at the time.
The WSL Big Wave Awards recognize the largest and most committed waves surfed each year across the globe.
Surfing competitions at the 2024 Paris Olympics were held at Teahupo'o in Tahiti, French Polynesia, over 15,000 kilometers from Paris.
The shaka sign, a hand gesture with thumb and pinky extended, originated in Hawaii and became a universal symbol of surf culture and aloha spirit.
Rail-to-rail surfing refers to transitioning smoothly from one edge (rail) of the board to the other during turns, a hallmark of high-performance surfing.
Rob Machado, known for his smooth style and environmental activism, is one of the most beloved surfers in the sport's history.
Dane Reynolds is widely regarded as one of the most talented free surfers ever, known for his progressive aerial game and creative approach.
Noseriding is a longboard technique where the surfer walks to the nose of the board and hangs five or ten toes over the tip.
The first surfing contest ever recorded was held in 1928 at Corona del Mar, California.
Waimea Bay on Oahu's North Shore was the birthplace of modern big wave surfing in the late 1950s, when Greg Noll and others first tackled its massive winter swells.
Rip Curl, founded in 1969 in Torquay, Australia, is one of the world's largest surf companies, producing wetsuits, apparel, and accessories.
The duck dive is a technique where a surfer pushes the nose of the board underwater to get beneath an incoming wave while paddling out.
Tyler Wright won two consecutive WSL Women's World Championship titles in 2016 and 2017.
The North Shore of Oahu, Hawaii, is known as the 'Seven Mile Miracle' for its concentration of world-class surf breaks along a short stretch of coastline.
Surf forecasting relies on meteorological data including wind speed and direction, swell height, swell period, and tide charts.
Mark Richards, nicknamed 'MR,' won four consecutive world titles from 1979 to 1982 and helped popularize the twin-fin surfboard design.
The bottom turn is considered the most fundamental maneuver in surfing, setting up the surfer's speed and trajectory for the rest of the wave.
Eddie Aikau was a legendary Hawaiian big wave surfer and lifeguard at Waimea Bay who never lost a single person during his tenure.
Backdoor Pipeline refers to riding the right-hand barrel at Pipeline, which breaks to the right of the main left-hand peak.
Quiksilver, founded in 1969 in Torquay, Australia, grew from a small boardshort company into one of the largest surf brands in the world.
The aerial, or air, involves launching off the lip of a wave into the air and landing back on the wave face, and has become a standard move in modern competitive surfing.
Mundaka in the Basque Country, Spain, is famous for its long, hollow left-hand rivermouth barrel.
Surf photography became an art form in the 1960s with pioneers like LeRoy Grannis and Ron Stoner documenting California's surf scene.
Kelly Slater became the youngest world champion in surfing history at age 20 in 1992.
A closeout wave is one that breaks all at once along its entire length, making it impossible to ride along the face.
The Billabong Pro Pipeline is one of the most prestigious events on the WSL Championship Tour, drawing the best tube riders in the world.
Padang Padang in Bali produces a short, intense barrel that only breaks properly on larger swells, making it one of the most challenging waves in Indonesia.
Foam boards, also called soft-tops, are the most popular boards for beginners due to their buoyancy, stability, and safety.
The cutback is a fundamental surfing maneuver where the surfer redirects back toward the breaking part of the wave to maintain speed and position.
Lisa Andersen won four consecutive world titles from 1994 to 1997 and is credited with transforming women's surfing.
Sandbars are underwater accumulations of sand that cause waves to break; their shape and position determine wave quality at beach breaks.
The Quiksilver Pro Gold Coast in Snapper Rocks, Australia, traditionally opens the WSL Championship Tour season.
Sebastian Steudtner of Germany surfed an 86-foot wave at Nazaré in 2020, setting a Guinness World Record for the largest wave ever surfed.
Surfboard shapers are craftspeople who hand-shape foam blanks into custom surfboards, and shaping is considered both a trade and an art.
The green room is the slang term for the inside of a barrel or tube, one of the most exhilarating experiences in surfing.
Nat Young of Australia won the 1966 World Surfing Championship and helped usher in the shortboard revolution.
Off-the-lip is a turn performed at the top of the wave where the surfer redirects off the breaking lip and comes back down the wave face.
Pipeline's reef is made of sharp, shallow lava rock, and wipeouts can result in serious injuries, making it one of the most dangerous waves to surf.
The International Surfing Association (ISA) is the world governing body for surfing, recognized by the International Olympic Committee.
La Jolla, California, is home to Windansea Beach, a legendary surf spot that was central to California's surf culture in the 1960s.
Surfing etiquette includes the drop-in rule, which states that the surfer closest to the breaking part of the wave has priority.
In 2017, Rodrigo Koxa surfed an 80-foot wave at Nazaré, setting a world record that has since been broken.
The turtle roll is a technique used by longboarders to get past broken waves by flipping the board upside down and holding on while the wave passes over.
Margaret River in Western Australia is known for powerful waves and has hosted WSL Championship Tour events since 2014.
The stringer is a thin strip of wood running down the center of a surfboard, providing strength and flex control.
Lakey Peterson won the 2019 US Open of Surfing and has been a consistent force on the WSL Championship Tour.
Dawn patrol refers to the practice of surfing at first light, when conditions are often glassy and crowds are minimal.
The snap, or re-entry, is a sharp, powerful turn at the top of the wave, redirecting the board back down the face.
Greg Noll, known as 'Da Bull,' was one of the first surfers to ride the massive waves at Waimea Bay in the late 1950s.
Offshore winds blow from the land toward the sea, grooming wave faces and creating ideal surfing conditions.
The Quiksilver in Memory of Eddie Aikau big wave event requires 40-foot wave faces at Waimea Bay to run, and has only been held a handful of times.
Surfers use tide charts to plan their sessions, as tides affect wave shape and quality differently at each break.
Filipe Toledo of Brazil is known for his explosive aerial surfing and won multiple WSL Championship Tour events.
A hollow wave is one that pitches forward and creates a tube or barrel, highly prized by experienced surfers.
The first World Surfing Championship was held in 1964 at Manly Beach in Sydney, Australia.
Epoxy surfboards are lighter and more durable than traditional polyurethane boards, and have become increasingly popular since the 2000s.
Supertubos in Peniche, Portugal, is known as the 'European Pipeline' for its powerful, hollow beach break barrels.
Joel Parkinson won the 2012 WSL World Championship in a dramatic final event of the season at Pipeline.
The floater is a maneuver where the surfer rides along the top of a crumbling wave section before dropping back down.
Surf schools have proliferated worldwide since the 2000s, making the sport more accessible to beginners than ever before.
Mark Occhilupo, known as 'Occy,' made a remarkable comeback to win the 1999 World Championship title after years away from competitive surfing.
Onshore winds blow from the sea toward the land, creating choppy, less desirable surfing conditions.
The rocker of a surfboard refers to the curve from nose to tail; more rocker helps in steep, hollow waves while less rocker adds speed on flatter waves.
G-Land (Grajagan) in Java, Indonesia, is a remote jungle wave known for its long, powerful left-hand barrels.
A kook is surfing slang for an inexperienced or clueless surfer who doesn't follow etiquette or safety norms.
Tom Carroll of Australia was one of the first surfers to master the barrel at Pipeline, winning the Pipe Masters twice in the 1990s.
Surfboard wax comes in different temperature ratings: tropical, warm, cool, and cold water formulas each have different melting points.
The WSL introduced a mid-season cut in 2022, reducing the Championship Tour field after five events to heighten competition.
Skeleton Bay in Namibia produces one of the longest sand-bottom barrels in the world, with rides lasting up to two minutes.
The Vans US Open of Surfing at Huntington Beach attracts over 500,000 spectators each year, making it one of the largest action sports events globally.
Surfing in cold water requires wetsuits ranging from 3/2mm for mild temperatures to 6/5/4mm for frigid winter conditions.
Adriano de Souza (Mineirinho) of Brazil won the 2015 WSL World Championship after a dramatic final day at Pipeline.
The lip of a wave is the curling, breaking part at the top; many high-performance maneuvers are executed at or above the lip.
AI (Andy Irons) was known for his intense competitiveness and ability to surf Pipeline better than almost anyone in history.
Nias in Sumatra, Indonesia, is famous for its mechanical, perfectly shaped right-hand barrel breaking over a reef.
A quiver is a surfer's collection of surfboards, typically including various shapes and sizes for different wave conditions.
Gerry Lopez, known as 'Mr. Pipeline,' is regarded as one of the most graceful tube riders in surfing history.
Surfing is estimated to be practiced by over 35 million people worldwide across more than 160 countries.
The WSL Finals, introduced in 2021, is a one-day event where the top five men and top five women compete in a surf-off to determine the world champion.
Santa Cruz, California, is home to Steamer Lane, one of the most iconic surf spots on the U.S. West Coast.
The snap heard 'round the world was Gabriel Medina's celebration after winning his first world title in 2014 at Pipeline.
Tow-in surfing uses personal watercraft to tow surfers into waves too large and fast-moving to catch by paddling.
Blacks Beach in San Diego is known for its powerful beach break and is a favorite among experienced Southern California surfers.
The rooster tail is the spray of water thrown off the tail of a surfboard during a hard turn, often seen in high-performance surfing.
Surfboard blanks are the foam cores from which boards are shaped; Clark Foam was the dominant blank manufacturer until its sudden closure in 2005.
Wave height is measured differently in Hawaii, where surfers measure the back of the wave, resulting in sizes roughly half of the wave face measurement.
Maya Gabeira of Brazil holds the Guinness World Record for the largest wave ever surfed by a woman, an estimated 73.5-foot wave at Nazaré.
Snapper Rocks on the Gold Coast, Australia, produces a long, sand-bottom right-hand barrel created by an artificial sand-pumping system.
Shaun Tomson of South Africa won the 1977 World Championship and is known for revolutionizing tube riding at Pipeline.
A closeout set is a series of large waves that break all at once, catching surfers inside and often resulting in difficult hold-downs.
The chin strap on surfing helmets has become standard at big wave events following safety protocol improvements in the 2010s.
The tail of a surfboard comes in various shapes including squash, round, pin, swallow, and diamond, each affecting how the board turns and holds in waves.
Jordy Smith of South Africa has been one of the most powerful surfers on the WSL Championship Tour, known for his incredible backhand surfing at J-Bay.
Surf magazines like Surfer (founded 1960) and Surfing Magazine helped define surf culture and spread the sport's image worldwide.
In surfing, getting 'caught inside' means being stuck in the impact zone where waves are breaking, unable to paddle past them to the lineup.
The lineup is the area in the ocean where surfers wait for waves to approach, usually just beyond where the waves are breaking.
The twin-fin surfboard design offers a looser, faster feel compared to the traditional single-fin and the later thruster setup.
Sunset Beach on Oahu's North Shore is known for its powerful, shifting peaks and is considered one of the most challenging waves in the world.
Simon Anderson invented the thruster three-fin setup in 1981 and proved it by winning the Bells Beach contest.
Tahiti hosted the surfing events for the 2024 Paris Olympics at the legendary Teahupo'o break.
Wipeout is the term for falling off a surfboard while riding a wave, and can range from harmless tumbles to dangerous hold-downs.
The 'over the falls' wipeout occurs when a surfer is caught in the lip of a breaking wave and thrown with it as it crashes.
The WSL uses the Jeep Leader Jersey (yellow) to identify the current world rankings leader during Championship Tour events.
Artificial wave pools, such as Kelly Slater's Surf Ranch in Lemoore, California, can produce perfect, repeatable waves for surfing.
Mentawai Islands in Indonesia are a remote surf destination known for their perfect reef breaks including Macaronis, Lance's Right, and HT's.
The Maldives became a popular surf destination starting in the late 1990s, offering uncrowded waves in a tropical setting.
The concave bottom of a surfboard channels water flow to increase speed and lift through turns.
A tombstone occurs when a surfer wipes out in heavy surf and their board is dragged underwater, standing vertically in the water with only the tip visible.
Sally Fitzgibbons of Australia has been a perennial top contender on the WSL Championship Tour, known for her determination and competitive spirit.
Cross-stepping is a longboard technique where the surfer walks forward and backward on the board using a cross-over foot movement.
The WSL introduced equal prize money for men and women in 2019, a landmark decision for gender equality in professional surfing.
Bondi Beach in Sydney, Australia, is one of the most famous urban surf beaches in the world and a cultural icon.
A section is a part of the wave that the surfer must navigate; difficult sections include steep or closing parts of the wave face.
In 2000, Laird Hamilton surfed the 'Millennium Wave' at Teahupo'o, one of the most iconic big wave rides ever captured on camera.
The Billabong Pipe Masters has determined the world champion in its final event more times than any other contest on tour.
A reef break creates more consistent and predictable waves than a beach break because the underwater topography doesn't shift with storms and tides.
Matt Warshaw's The Encyclopedia of Surfing is considered the definitive reference work on the history of surfing and surf culture.
The drop is the initial moment of riding a wave when the surfer transitions from paddling to standing and descending the wave face.
Raglan in New Zealand is famous for its long left-hand point break and was featured in The Endless Summer.
The Outer Banks of North Carolina are one of the most consistent surf destinations on the U.S. East Coast, particularly during hurricane season.
Kelly Slater became the oldest world champion in surfing at age 39 in 2011.
The pearl is what happens when the nose of the board digs into the water during takeoff, often catapulting the surfer forward.
Thurso East in Scotland is one of the best reef breaks in Europe, producing powerful right-handers in frigid North Atlantic waters.
Competitive surfing uses a priority system where one surfer has the right to catch the next wave, preventing interference.
Barton Lynch of Australia won the 1988 World Championship title and has since become a prominent surf event commentator and organizer.
The pocket is the steepest, most powerful part of the wave, located just ahead of the breaking section, where the best surfers perform their most critical turns.
Boogie boarding (bodyboarding) is one of the most popular forms of wave riding, with participants lying prone or riding drop-knee on a shorter foam board.
Hawaii declared surfing its official state individual sport in 1998.
The WSL Challenger Series serves as a qualification pathway for surfers to earn spots on the elite Championship Tour.
Chicama in Peru is considered the longest left-hand point break in the world, with rides that can extend over two kilometers.
A section called a 'closeout section' is a part of the wave that breaks suddenly and requires the surfer to either go over it or straighten off.
John John Florence is from the North Shore of Oahu and grew up surfing Pipeline, which is visible from his family home.
The quad-fin setup uses four fins for a combination of speed and hold, particularly effective in hollow, powerful waves.
The Volcom Pipe Pro at Pipeline is one of the most prestigious specialty events in surfing.
Big wave surfers wear inflatable vests that can be manually triggered to bring them to the surface during dangerous hold-downs.
Swellnet, Surfline, and Magic Seaweed are popular surf forecasting websites that surfers use to plan sessions based on real-time data.
The pop-up is the quick motion of going from lying on the board to a standing position, and is one of the first skills beginners must master.
Jack Johnson, the singer-songwriter known for hits like 'Flake' and 'Banana Pancakes,' was a competitive surfer before turning to music.
The carve is a wide, arcing turn on the wave face, utilizing the surfboard's rail for speed and power.
Anchor Point in Morocco is North Africa's most famous surf spot, a long right-hand point break popular with European surfers.
Australia has produced more world surfing champions than any other country, with legends like Mark Richards, Tom Carroll, and Mick Fanning.
The impact zone is the area where waves break most forcefully, and getting caught in it can be one of the most dangerous situations in surfing.
CJ Hobgood won the 2001 WSL World Championship in one of the closest title races in tour history.
Localism is a sometimes aggressive territorial behavior where local surfers assert dominance over visiting surfers at their home breaks.
Cortes Bank, located about 100 miles off the coast of Southern California, is an underwater mountain that produces some of the largest rideable waves on Earth.
The WSL introduced a format of five-wave totals in heats, counting the best two wave scores, with a perfect heat total being 20 points.
Keramas in Bali is a right-hand reef break that has hosted WSL Championship Tour events due to its high-performance wave quality.
Poly (polyurethane/polyester) surfboards remain the most traditional construction method, prized by many surfers for their flex and feel.
Owen Wright of Australia returned to elite competition after suffering a traumatic brain injury from a wipeout at Pipeline, eventually winning the 2017 Quiksilver Pro.
The US Open of Surfing has been held at Huntington Beach since 1959, making it one of the longest-running surf contests in the world.
A channel is a deeper section of water next to a breaking wave, often used by surfers to paddle out without fighting the whitewater.
Riding with a goofy stance means leading with the right foot forward, while regular stance means leading with the left foot.
In bodyboarding, the drop-knee style involves riding with one foot planted on the board and the other knee dropped, allowing for more vertical maneuvers.
Fernando de Noronha in Brazil is an archipelago known for its crystal-clear water and quality right-hand reef breaks.
Torquay, Victoria, Australia, is considered the surfing capital of Australia and is home to the headquarters of Rip Curl and Quiksilver.
Surfline was one of the first online surf forecasting services, launching in the mid-1990s with cameras at popular surf breaks.
The fin box is the slot on the bottom of a surfboard where removable fins are attached, allowing surfers to swap fin configurations.
Peter Mel won the 2013 Big Wave World Tour title and is known for charging Mavericks.
A clean-up set is an unusually large set of waves that breaks further out than normal, catching surfers off guard in the lineup.
The alaia is an ancient Hawaiian surfboard made from a single plank of wood, typically koa or wiliwili, ridden finless.
Kelly Slater's Wave Company developed the Surf Ranch, an artificial wave pool in California's Central Valley that has hosted WSL events.
Wax combs are small tools used to create textured patterns in surf wax for better grip on the board's deck.
Japan has a passionate surfing community, with quality waves found in Chiba, Shikoku, Miyazaki, and throughout the country.
The World Surf League was originally called the Association of Surfing Professionals (ASP), rebranding to WSL in 2015.
Coxos in Ericeira, Portugal, is considered one of the best right-hand reef breaks in Europe and part of a World Surfing Reserve.
The Billabong XXL Awards (now the WSL Big Wave Awards) have recognized the largest waves surfed each year since 2000.
The standup barrel is one of surfing's most iconic images, with the surfer crouching inside a tube of water as the wave breaks around them.
Flat spells are periods when there are no significant waves to surf, often lasting days or weeks depending on the season and location.
The Surf Ranch Pro held at Kelly Slater's Surf Ranch in Lemoore became a Championship Tour event in 2018.
Mick Fanning won three WSL World Championship titles in 2007, 2009, and 2013.
Lennox Head in New South Wales, Australia, is a spectacular right-hand point break that produces long, workable walls.
In surfing, a section is rated 'critical' when it demands the highest degree of commitment and skill from the surfer.
The first known surf club was founded in 1908 on Waikiki Beach in Honolulu and was called the Outrigger Canoe Club.
A foam ball is the churning, aerated whitewater behind a breaking wave section.
Mark Healey is one of the most respected big wave surfers, known for riding the heaviest waves at Jaws and Teahupo'o.
Surfing generates an estimated $50 billion in global economic activity annually when including tourism, equipment, apparel, and media.
Caroline Marks of the USA became one of the youngest surfers to qualify for the WSL Championship Tour at age 16.
Erosion and sand replenishment projects can significantly change the quality of beach break surf spots over time.
The concept of 'dropping in' or 'snaking' another surfer's wave is one of the most serious breaches of surf etiquette.
Kirra Point on the Gold Coast, Australia, was once considered the world's best barrel but was severely affected by sand-pumping operations.
Hatteras Island in the Outer Banks of North Carolina receives consistent hurricane swell and is one of the best surf destinations on the East Coast.
Sunny Garcia won the 2000 WSL World Championship after years of being one of the most powerful surfers on tour.
A two-wave total is the scoring system used in WSL competitive heats, where only the two highest-scoring rides count.
The barrel roll is an advanced bodyboarding maneuver where the rider spins 360 degrees inside the tube of a wave.
Haleiwa on Oahu's North Shore produces a challenging, multi-section wave and is a traditional event site for Hawaiian surfing contests.
Wavegarden and Surf Lakes are companies developing wave pool technology that could bring surfing to landlocked areas worldwide.
The single-fin surfboard was the standard design from the sport's origins through the 1970s before multi-fin setups emerged.
Mark Richards pioneered the twin-fin revolution in the late 1970s, winning four world titles on a design that was considered radical at the time.
Ericeira, Portugal, was designated a World Surfing Reserve in 2011 for its exceptional quality and density of surf breaks.
Dropping your board in front of another surfer who is paddling out can cause serious injuries and is a major safety concern.
The Billabong Pro Tahiti at Teahupo'o is considered the most intense event on the WSL Championship Tour.
The term 'stoked' originated in surf culture and means to be extremely excited or enthusiastic.
New Smyrna Beach, Florida, has been called the shark bite capital of the world, yet remains one of the most popular surf spots on the East Coast.
The WSL has introduced helmet recommendations for surfers competing in heavy waves like Pipeline and Teahupo'o.
Bruce Brown's The Endless Summer premiered on June 15, 1966, and became a crossover hit that brought surfing to mainstream audiences.
Boardshorts evolved from basic swim trunks into a surf staple; the quick-drying, stretch-fabric designs now worn were pioneered by companies like Quiksilver and Billabong.
Puerto Escondido in Mexico, known as the 'Mexican Pipeline,' produces one of the heaviest beach break barrels in the world.
A down-the-line wave is one that allows the surfer to ride parallel to the shore along the face, as opposed to riding straight toward the beach.
The Vissla Sydney Surf Pro at Manly Beach has been a launching pad for WSL Challenger Series competitors.
Kai Lenny is a modern waterman from Maui who excels at surfing, big wave surfing, foiling, standup paddle, and windsurfing.
Gonçalo Vasconcelos, Alex Botelho, and Lucas Chumbo are among the brave big wave surfers who regularly challenge the giants at Nazaré.
The impact on coral reefs from surfing-related development has become an environmental concern in popular tropical surf destinations.
The fin-first takeoff at Teahupo'o, where surfers drop in going backward due to the wave's intensity, is one of the most extreme maneuvers in surfing.
In the early 1900s, surfboards could weigh over 100 pounds and were made from solid wood, making maneuvers nearly impossible.
The Superbank on the Gold Coast of Australia is a man-made sand formation that created a connected Snapper Rocks-to-Kirra wave stretching nearly two kilometers.
The Rip Curl WSL Finals have been held at Lower Trestles (San Clemente, California) since the format was introduced in 2021.
Dane Kealoha was a powerful Hawaiian surfer who won the 1980 Duke Kahanamoku Classic and was known for his backhand barrel riding.
A mushburger is a slow, crumbling wave that lacks power and steepness, generally not ideal for high-performance surfing.
Tom Morey, inventor of the boogie board, was originally a surfer and mathematical engineer who created the bodyboard accidentally in his garage.
The WSL Qualifying Series (now Challenger Series) is the feeder tour where surfers compete for spots on the elite Championship Tour.
Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, has a vibrant urban surf scene with breaks like Arpoador and Prainha drawing surfers year-round.
Surfing's environmental movement has grown significantly, with organizations like Surfrider Foundation and Save The Waves Coalition advocating for ocean protection.
The Surfrider Foundation was founded in 1984 in Malibu, California, to protect the world's oceans, waves, and beaches.
A walled-up wave is one that is steep but not breaking, forming a solid wall of water that is difficult to generate speed on.
The International Surfing Day is celebrated annually on the third Saturday of June.
Tim Bonython is an Australian surf filmmaker known for his big wave documentaries and incredible footage of Jaws and other breaks.
Croyde Bay in Devon, England, is one of the most consistent surf spots in the UK, producing quality beach break waves.
Concussion protocols and safety jet ski teams have become mandatory at all WSL Championship Tour events featuring heavy waves.
The floater originated in the 1980s when surfers like Martin Potter began riding over crumbling wave sections rather than turning on them.
Sri Lanka's Arugam Bay has become a famous surf destination, attracting surfers from around the world with its consistent right-hand point break.
The Volcom brand was founded by Richard Woolcott and Tucker Hall in 1991, blending skateboarding, snowboarding, and surfing culture.
Wave pools capable of producing surf-quality waves use various technologies including pneumatic chambers, plunging hulls, and mechanical paddles.
A layback is a maneuver where the surfer lays back onto the wave face while making a turn over a steep section, with their back touching the water.
Baleal Island in Peniche, Portugal, offers 270-degree swell exposure, meaning surfers can almost always find a wave regardless of swell direction.
Duke Kahanamoku demonstrated surfing in Australia at Freshwater Beach, Sydney, on December 24, 1914, introducing the sport to the country.
The channel bottom design on a surfboard features parallel channels on the underside that direct water flow for increased speed and lift.
Mikala Jones, a Hawaiian tube-riding specialist, was known for his incredible barrel photography using GoPro cameras mounted inside the wave.
A splitpeak is a wave that breaks in both directions from a central point, offering both a right and a left.
Softech, Catch Surf, and Wavestorm are popular soft-top surfboard brands that have helped make surfing more accessible to beginners.
Noosa Heads in Queensland, Australia, features five right-hand point breaks along its national park coastline.
The WSL Mid-Season Cut, introduced in 2022, eliminates the bottom portion of the Championship Tour field at the halfway point of the season.
A channel island is not just a surfboard brand; it refers to any island off a coastline that can influence swell direction and wave quality.
The Fiji Pro at Cloudbreak and Restaurants was one of the most spectacular events on the WSL Championship Tour before it was dropped from the calendar.
The aerial game in competitive surfing has advanced dramatically, with full rotations, inverted airs, and rodeo flips becoming common in competition.
Gabriel Medina became the first surfer to land a backflip in competitive surfing at the 2015 Fiji Pro.
Slab waves are extremely heavy, thick-lipped waves caused by sudden depth changes in the ocean floor, such as at Teahupo'o and The Right in Western Australia.
Rob Machado's Seaside model surfboard helped popularize the fish-style board for everyday surfing conditions.
Biarritz, France, is considered the birthplace of European surfing, where the sport was introduced in the late 1950s.
The impact vest (flotation vest) has become standard safety equipment for big wave surfers, providing buoyancy during wipeouts.
Freddy Patacchia, Sunny Garcia, and Derek Ho are Hawaiian surfers who brought power surfing to the forefront of competitive surfing.
Peniche, Portugal, hosts the MEO Rip Curl Pro Portugal, a staple event on the WSL Championship Tour.
The glassing process involves applying fiberglass cloth and resin over a shaped foam blank to create a hardened, waterproof shell.
El Porto in Manhattan Beach, California, is a popular and consistent beach break in the South Bay area of Los Angeles.
Courtney Conlogue of the USA has been a fierce competitor on the WSL Championship Tour, known for her powerful surfing style.
The late takeoff is a high-risk maneuver where a surfer catches the wave as late as possible, dropping into a steep, critical section.
Cornwall, England, is the heart of British surfing, with famous spots including Fistral Beach, Watergate Bay, and Sennen Cove.
The WSL's CT events are broadcast live online, allowing surf fans worldwide to watch competition in real time.
Dane Reynolds' video parts in Taylor Steele films are considered some of the most progressive surfing footage ever captured.
Surf therapy programs use surfing to help veterans, at-risk youth, and people with disabilities improve mental and physical health.
A bonzer is a surfboard design created by the Campbell brothers in the 1970s, featuring a unique concave bottom with side runners.
The Red Bull Cape Fear event at Shipstern Bluff in Tasmania features one of the most dangerous and mutant waves in the world.
Surf travel has become a multi-billion dollar industry, with surf resorts, charter boats, and guided trips in over 100 countries.
The Clark Foam factory closure in December 2005 caused a major crisis in the surfboard industry and accelerated the adoption of alternative blank materials.
A wahine is the Hawaiian word for a female surfer, and the term is commonly used in surf culture worldwide.
The world's largest surfboard, measuring over 42 feet, was ridden by 66 surfers simultaneously in Huntington Beach in 2015.
Aritz Aranburu of Spain was the first Basque surfer to compete on the WSL Championship Tour, inspiring a generation of European surfers.
Glass-on fins are permanently attached to the board and cannot be removed, offering a direct, responsive feel preferred by some surfers.
The stall is a technique where a surfer deliberately slows down on a wave to allow the barrel to catch up and cover them.
Pichilemu in Chile is considered the surfing capital of South America, known for its powerful left-hand point break called Punta de Lobos.
Wavegarden's Cove technology was used to create The Wave in Bristol, England, one of the first commercial-scale artificial surf parks in Europe.
The ISA World Surfing Games have been held annually since 1964 and serve as the qualifying pathway for Olympic surfing.
A switchfoot surfer can ride both regular and goofy stance, which is extremely rare and provides an advantage in varying wave directions.
Derek Ho of Hawaii became the first Hawaiian to win the world title in 1993, a huge milestone for Hawaiian surfing.
The Da Hui Backdoor Shootout at Pipeline is an invitation-only event that celebrates Hawaiian big wave surfing.
Surfboard ding repair involves patching cracks and holes in the fiberglass shell with resin and cloth to keep the board waterproof.
The total global surf industry, including equipment, apparel, tourism, and media, is estimated to be worth over $10 billion annually.
Kolohe Andino of San Clemente, California, is one of the leading American surfers on the WSL Championship Tour.
Ryan 'Hippo' Hipwood is an Australian big wave bodyboarder known for charging massive waves at Jaws and Nazaré.
A wind swell is generated by local winds and tends to produce shorter-period, less organized waves compared to groundswell.
The Triple Crown of Surfing on the North Shore of Oahu consists of three events at Haleiwa, Sunset Beach, and Pipeline.
The shoulder of a wave is the unbroken, tapering part of the wave face adjacent to the breaking section, where turns are often initiated.
Billabong, founded in 1973 by Gordon Merchant on the Gold Coast, grew from a backyard business sewing boardshorts into a global surf brand.
Moore's Beach in Tasmania was where Shipstern Bluff was first surfed, an incredibly thick and mutant-shaped wave.
Jack Robinson of Australia has emerged as one of the best barrel riders in the world at both Pipeline and Margaret River.
The wave face is the unbroken part of the wave that the surfer rides on; measuring the face gives a larger number than measuring the back.
Tavarua Island in Fiji was a private surf resort that controlled access to Cloudbreak and Restaurants until Fiji's government opened the waves to all in 2010.
Ian Cairns and Peter Townend helped establish the professional surfing tour in the 1970s as founding members of the IPS.
The wraparound cutback, or roundhouse cutback, involves a full carving turn back toward the curl followed by a re-entry off the whitewater.
Surfing was practiced by Hawaiian royalty, with chiefs (ali'i) having exclusive access to the best waves and the finest boards.
The priority system in WSL events means one surfer in each heat has the right to catch the next wave, rotating after each ride.
The Wedge in Newport Beach, California, produces radical shorebreak waves caused by wave energy reflecting off the harbor jetty.
Dusty Payne, Matt Meola, and Albee Layer are Maui-based aerial specialists who have pushed the boundaries of above-the-lip surfing.
Wool wetsuits were the earliest form of thermal protection for surfers before Jack O'Neill developed the neoprene wetsuit.
Italo Ferreira grew up surfing on a cooler lid in Baía Formosa, Brazil, before becoming an Olympic gold medalist and world champion.
The Victoria World Bodyboarding Championship has been held at Pipeline, showcasing the best bodyboarders in the world.
A bumpy wave, created by cross-shore or onshore winds, makes it difficult for surfers to maintain speed and execute clean turns.
Taylor Steele is one of the most influential surf filmmakers, having directed films like Momentum, Sipping Jetstreams, and Innersection.
Wiggolly Dantas and Jadson Andre are Brazilian surfers who have helped build Brazil's powerhouse presence on the WSL Championship Tour.
The first professional surfing world tour was the International Professional Surfers (IPS), established in 1976.
Pipeline in Hawaii has two main surf spots: the Banzai Pipeline (lefts) and Backdoor (rights), both breaking over the same reef.
The North Shore of Oahu swells primarily between November and February, when large North Pacific storms generate massive surf.
Bethany Hamilton's story was told in the 2011 film Soul Surfer, starring AnnaSophia Robb.
The WSL's average heat duration is 30 minutes for round-of-32 heats and 35 minutes for later rounds.
Manly Beach in Sydney was where Duke Kahanamoku first demonstrated surfing to Australians over 100 years ago.
Michel Bourez of Tahiti is known as the 'Spartan' and has been one of the top French Polynesian surfers on the WSL Championship Tour.
The alley-oop is an aerial maneuver where the surfer rotates in the opposite direction of their travel, going against the spin of the wave.
The golden ratio to wave period relationship is why experienced surfers know that swells with periods over 15 seconds produce the best waves.
San Sebastián in Spain's Basque Country has become a vibrant European surf city, with breaks like Zurriola Beach attracting surfers year-round.
Craig Anderson from New South Wales is considered one of the most stylish free surfers in the modern era.
International Surfing Day was established by Surfing Magazine and the Surfrider Foundation in 2004.
The blank is the shaped piece of polyurethane or EPS foam that becomes the core of a surfboard before it is glassed.
Pedro Scooby is a Brazilian big wave surfer who regularly charges Nazaré's monster waves.
A surfer's local break is the wave they surf most often and know most intimately, from its sandbars to its moods.
The Sunset Beach Pro is a longstanding contest on the North Shore that challenges surfers with its powerful, shifting peaks.
Santa Cruz was one of the first places on mainland America where surfing was practiced, with records dating back to the early 1900s.
The WSL introduced a one-day Finals format in 2021, where the top-ranked surfers compete head-to-head for the world title at a single location.
Indonesia's surf season runs from April to October, when consistent swells from the Indian Ocean hit the archipelago's west-facing coastlines.
Layne Beachley of Australia won seven world titles between 1998 and 2006, making her one of the most dominant women's surfers in history.
A rip current can be identified by a channel of choppy, discolored water flowing away from the shore between breaking waves.
Taj Burrow of Australia was a stylish and versatile surfer who spent 18 years on the WSL Championship Tour.
The take-off zone is the specific area on a wave where surfers begin their ride, which varies depending on wave size and type.
The Triple Crown of Surfing was first awarded in 1983, combining results from three marquee events on the North Shore.
The Clark Foam monopoly meant that at its peak, over 90% of surfboards worldwide were shaped from Clark Foam blanks.
Pipe bodyboarder Mike Stewart has won more Pipeline events than any surfer or bodyboarder in history, earning the nickname 'Mr. Pipeline of bodyboarding.'
Wave pools like URBNSURF in Melbourne, Australia, allow inland communities to experience surfing without access to the ocean.
The Quemao Class big wave event in Lanzarote, Canary Islands, showcases one of the heaviest left-hand slabs in the Atlantic.
Keala Kennelly became the first woman to be tow-surfed at Jaws and later won the 2016 WSL Big Wave Award for Women's Best Performance.
Patagonia, originally an outdoor clothing company, became a major player in surf apparel with its Yulex plant-based wetsuits.
The Maui Pro at Honolua Bay showcases one of the most beautiful right-hand point breaks in the Hawaiian Islands.
Groms are young surfers, typically under 16, and grom competitions serve as the proving ground for future professional surfers.
Surfboard design has evolved from 10-foot solid wood planks to ultra-light composite boards weighing as little as 5 pounds.
The Basque Country in Spain and France has emerged as one of Europe's premier surfing regions with world-class waves at Mundaka, Hossegor, and Belharra.
Night surfing under floodlights has gained popularity at select locations, adding a surreal dimension to the wave-riding experience.
The rail grab is a tube-riding technique where the surfer grabs the outside rail of the board to maintain stability inside the barrel.
